question: 5. Show that the roots of \( x^{4}+a x^{3}+b x^{2}+c x+d=0 \), if suitably numbered, satisfv the relation \( \left.r_{1} / r\right)=r_{3} / r_{4} \). provided \( a^{2} d=c^{2} \neq 0 \)
solution:
Solution. We shall show that, for any quartic (1) with roots \( r_{1}, r_{2}, r_{3}, r_{4} \), we have
\[
\left(r_{1} r_{2}-r_{3} r_{4}\right)\left(r_{1} r_{3}-r_{4} r_{2}\right)\left(r_{1} r_{4}-r_{2} r_{3}\right)=a^{2} d-c^{2} .
\]

Multiplying out the left member, we obtain
\[
\Sigma r_{1}{ }^{3} r_{2} r_{3} r_{4}-\Sigma r_{1}{ }^{2} r_{2}{ }^{2} r_{3}{ }^{2},
\]
where in each case the sum is over the distinct terms obtained by permuting the subscripts. On the other hand, since
\[
a=-\Sigma r_{1}, \quad c=-\Sigma r_{1} r_{2} r_{3}, \quad \text { and } d=r_{1} r_{2} r_{3} r_{4},
\]
we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
a^{2} d-c^{2} & =\left(\Sigma r_{1}^{2}+2 \Sigma r_{1} r_{2}\right) r_{1} r_{2} r_{3} r_{4}-\left(\sum r_{1}^{2} r_{2}^{2} r_{3}^{2}+2 \Sigma r_{1}^{2} r_{2}^{2} r_{3} r_{4}\right) \\
& =\Sigma r_{1}^{3} r_{2} r_{3} r_{4}-\Sigma r_{1}^{2} r_{2}^{2} r_{3}^{2} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus (2) is established.
Given that \( a^{2} d=c^{2} \), we know that
\[
\left(r_{1} r_{2}-r_{3} r_{4}\right)\left(r_{1} r_{3}-r_{4} r_{2}\right)\left(r_{1} r_{4}-r_{2} r_{3}\right)=0,
\]
so one of the factors must vanish. By renumbering the roots we can arrange that
\[
r_{1} r_{4}-r_{2} r_{3}=0 .
\]

Since \( d \neq 0 \), none of the roots vanish, so we can divide by \( r_{2} r_{4} \) to obtain
\[
\frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}=\frac{r_{3}}{r_{4}}
\]
as required.

descriptive_long.question: 5. Show that the roots of \( unknownx^{4}+coefficienta\, unknownx^{3}+coefficientb\, unknownx^{2}+coefficientc\, unknownx+coefficientd=0 \), if suitably numbered, satisfv the relation \( \left.firstroot / r\right)=thirdroot / fourthroot \). provided \( coefficienta^{2} coefficientd=coefficientc^{2} \neq 0 \)
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. We shall show that, for any quartic (1) with roots \( firstroot, secondroot, thirdroot, fourthroot \), we have
\[
\left(firstroot secondroot-thirdroot fourthroot\right)\left(firstroot thirdroot-fourthroot secondroot\right)\left(firstroot fourthroot-secondroot thirdroot\right)=coefficienta^{2} coefficientd-coefficientc^{2} .
\]

Multiplying out the left member, we obtain
\[
\Sigma firstroot{ }^{3} secondroot thirdroot fourthroot-\Sigma firstroot{ }^{2} secondroot{ }^{2} thirdroot{ }^{2},
\]
where in each case the sum is over the distinct terms obtained by permuting the subscripts. On the other hand, since
\[
coefficienta=-\Sigma firstroot, \quad coefficientc=-\Sigma firstroot secondroot thirdroot, \quad \text { and } coefficientd=firstroot secondroot thirdroot fourthroot,
\]
we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
coefficienta^{2} coefficientd-coefficientc^{2} & =\left(\Sigma firstroot^{2}+2 \Sigma firstroot secondroot\right) firstroot secondroot thirdroot fourthroot-\left(\sum firstroot^{2} secondroot^{2} thirdroot^{2}+2 \Sigma firstroot^{2} secondroot^{2} thirdroot fourthroot\right) \\
& =\Sigma firstroot^{3} secondroot thirdroot fourthroot-\Sigma firstroot^{2} secondroot^{2} thirdroot^{2} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus (2) is established.
Given that \( coefficienta^{2} coefficientd=coefficientc^{2} \), we know that
\[
\left(firstroot secondroot-thirdroot fourthroot\right)\left(firstroot thirdroot-fourthroot secondroot\right)\left(firstroot fourthroot-secondroot thirdroot\right)=0,
\]
so one of the factors must vanish. By renumbering the roots we can arrange that
\[
firstroot fourthroot-secondroot thirdroot=0 .
\]

Since \( coefficientd \neq 0 \), none of the roots vanish, so we can divide by \( secondroot fourthroot \) to obtain
\[
\frac{firstroot}{secondroot}=\frac{thirdroot}{fourthroot}
\]
as required.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 5. Show that the roots of \( buttercup^{4}+hummingbird buttercup^{3}+pinecone buttercup^{2}+scarecrow buttercup+paintbrush=0 \), if suitably numbered, satisfv the relation \( \left.chandelier / r\right)=windmill / snowflake \). provided \( hummingbird^{2} paintbrush=scarecrow^{2} \neq 0 \)
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. We shall show that, for any quartic (1) with roots \( chandelier, marshmallow, windmill, snowflake \), we have
\[
\left(chandelier marshmallow-windmill snowflake\right)\left(chandelier windmill-snowflake marshmallow\right)\left(chandelier snowflake-marshmallow windmill\right)=hummingbird^{2} paintbrush-scarecrow^{2} .
\]

Multiplying out the left member, we obtain
\[
\Sigma chandelier{ }^{3} marshmallow windmill snowflake-\Sigma chandelier{ }^{2} marshmallow{ }^{2} windmill{ }^{2},
\]
where in each case the sum is over the distinct terms obtained by permuting the subscripts. On the other hand, since
\[
hummingbird=-\Sigma chandelier, \quad scarecrow=-\Sigma chandelier marshmallow windmill, \quad \text { and } paintbrush=chandelier marshmallow windmill snowflake,
\]
we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
hummingbird^{2} paintbrush-scarecrow^{2} & =\left(\Sigma chandelier^{2}+2 \Sigma chandelier marshmallow\right) chandelier marshmallow windmill snowflake-\left(\sum chandelier^{2} marshmallow^{2} windmill^{2}+2 \Sigma chandelier^{2} marshmallow^{2} windmill snowflake\right) \\
& =\Sigma chandelier^{3} marshmallow windmill snowflake-\Sigma chandelier^{2} marshmallow^{2} windmill^{2} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus (2) is established.
Given that \( hummingbird^{2} paintbrush=scarecrow^{2} \), we know that
\[
\left(chandelier marshmallow-windmill snowflake\right)\left(chandelier windmill-snowflake marshmallow\right)\left(chandelier snowflake-marshmallow windmill\right)=0,
\]
so one of the factors must vanish. By renumbering the roots we can arrange that
\[
chandelier snowflake-marshmallow windmill=0 .
\]

Since \( paintbrush \neq 0 \), none of the roots vanish, so we can divide by \( marshmallow snowflake \) to obtain
\[
\frac{chandelier}{marshmallow}=\frac{windmill}{snowflake}
\]
as required.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: 5. Show that the roots of \( knownconstant^{4}+changingalpha knownconstant^{3}+changingbeta knownconstant^{2}+changinggamma knownconstant+changingdelta=0 \), if suitably numbered, satisfv the relation \( \left.leafnumberone / r\right)=leafnumberthree / leafnumberfour \). provided \( changingalpha^{2} changingdelta=changinggamma^{2} \neq 0 \)
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. We shall show that, for any quartic (1) with roots \( leafnumberone, leafnumbertwo, leafnumberthree, leafnumberfour \), we have
\[
\left(leafnumberone leafnumbertwo-leafnumberthree leafnumberfour\right)\left(leafnumberone leafnumberthree-leafnumberfour leafnumbertwo\right)\left(leafnumberone leafnumberfour-leafnumbertwo leafnumberthree\right)=changingalpha^{2} changingdelta-changinggamma^{2} .
\]

Multiplying out the left member, we obtain
\[
\Sigma leafnumberone{ }^{3} leafnumbertwo leafnumberthree leafnumberfour-\Sigma leafnumberone{ }^{2} leafnumbertwo{ }^{2} leafnumberthree{ }^{2},
\]
where in each case the sum is over the distinct terms obtained by permuting the subscripts. On the other hand, since
\[
changingalpha=-\Sigma leafnumberone, \quad changinggamma=-\Sigma leafnumberone leafnumbertwo leafnumberthree, \quad \text { and } changingdelta=leafnumberone leafnumbertwo leafnumberthree leafnumberfour,
\]
we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
changingalpha^{2} changingdelta-changinggamma^{2} & =\left(\Sigma leafnumberone^{2}+2 \Sigma leafnumberone leafnumbertwo\right) leafnumberone leafnumbertwo leafnumberthree leafnumberfour-\left(\sum leafnumberone^{2} leafnumbertwo^{2} leafnumberthree^{2}+2 \Sigma leafnumberone^{2} leafnumbertwo^{2} leafnumberthree leafnumberfour\right) \\
& =\Sigma leafnumberone^{3} leafnumbertwo leafnumberthree leafnumberfour-\Sigma leafnumberone^{2} leafnumbertwo^{2} leafnumberthree^{2} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus (2) is established.
Given that \( changingalpha^{2} changingdelta=changinggamma^{2} \), we know that
\[
\left(leafnumberone leafnumbertwo-leafnumberthree leafnumberfour\right)\left(leafnumberone leafnumberthree-leafnumberfour leafnumbertwo\right)\left(leafnumberone leafnumberfour-leafnumbertwo leafnumberthree\right)=0,
\]
so one of the factors must vanish. By renumbering the roots we can arrange that
\[
leafnumberone leafnumberfour-leafnumbertwo leafnumberthree=0 .
\]

Since \( changingdelta \neq 0 \), none of the roots vanish, so we can divide by \( leafnumbertwo leafnumberfour \) to obtain
\[
\frac{leafnumberone}{leafnumbertwo}=\frac{leafnumberthree}{leafnumberfour}
\]
as required.

garbled_string.question: 5. Show that the roots of \( mqpwzthj^{4}+kefumiqs mqpwzthj^{3}+durpsnva mqpwzthj^{2}+tahjzxel mqpwzthj+opvinkwr=0 \), if suitably numbered, satisfv the relation \( \left.zlnqkstu / r\right)=cjtsuaph / wdkyolvi \). provided \( kefumiqs^{2} opvinkwr=tahjzxel^{2} \neq 0 \)
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. We shall show that, for any quartic (1) with roots \( zlnqkstu, fvyxrbem, cjtsuaph, wdkyolvi \), we have
\[
\left(zlnqkstu fvyxrbem-cjtsuaph wdkyolvi\right)\left(zlnqkstu cjtsuaph-wdkyolvi fvyxrbem\right)\left(zlnqkstu wdkyolvi-fvyxrbem cjtsuaph\right)=kefumiqs^{2} opvinkwr-tahjzxel^{2} .
\]

Multiplying out the left member, we obtain
\[
\Sigma zlnqkstu{ }^{3} fvyxrbem cjtsuaph wdkyolvi-\Sigma zlnqkstu{ }^{2} fvyxrbem{ }^{2} cjtsuaph{ }^{2},
\]
where in each case the sum is over the distinct terms obtained by permuting the subscripts. On the other hand, since
\[
kefumiqs=-\Sigma zlnqkstu, \quad tahjzxel=-\Sigma zlnqkstu fvyxrbem cjtsuaph, \quad \text { and } opvinkwr=zlnqkstu fvyxrbem cjtsuaph wdkyolvi,
\]
we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
kefumiqs^{2} opvinkwr-tahjzxel^{2} & =\left(\Sigma zlnqkstu^{2}+2 \Sigma zlnqkstu fvyxrbem\right) zlnqkstu fvyxrbem cjtsuaph wdkyolvi-\left(\sum zlnqkstu^{2} fvyxrbem^{2} cjtsuaph^{2}+2 \Sigma zlnqkstu^{2} fvyxrbem^{2} cjtsuaph wdkyolvi\right) \\
& =\Sigma zlnqkstu^{3} fvyxrbem cjtsuaph wdkyolvi-\Sigma zlnqkstu^{2} fvyxrbem^{2} cjtsuaph^{2} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus (2) is established.
Given that \( kefumiqs^{2} opvinkwr=tahjzxel^{2} \), we know that
\[
\left(zlnqkstu fvyxrbem-cjtsuaph wdkyolvi\right)\left(zlnqkstu cjtsuaph-wdkyolvi fvyxrbem\right)\left(zlnqkstu wdkyolvi-fvyxrbem cjtsuaph\right)=0,
\]
so one of the factors must vanish. By renumbering the roots we can arrange that
\[
zlnqkstu wdkyolvi-fvyxrbem cjtsuaph=0 .
\]

Since \( opvinkwr \neq 0 \), none of the roots vanish, so we can divide by \( fvyxrbem wdkyolvi \) to obtain
\[
\frac{zlnqkstu}{fvyxrbem}=\frac{cjtsuaph}{wdkyolvi}
\]
as required.

kernel_variant.question:
Let the quartic polynomial
	x^{4}+p x^{3}+q x^{2}+r x+s=0
have (not necessarily distinct) complex roots r_{1},r_{2},r_{3},r_{4}.  Assume that
	p^{2}s=r^{2}\neq 0.
Show that, after a suitable renumbering of the roots,
	r_{1}/r_{4}=r_{3}/r_{2}.
kernel_variant.solution:
Set
	P=(r_{1}r_{2}-r_{3}r_{4})\,(r_{1}r_{3}-r_{2}r_{4})\,(r_{1}r_{4}-r_{2}r_{3}).

1.  We first express P in terms of the elementary symmetric sums of the roots.
   By Vieta's formulas for x^{4}+p x^{3}+q x^{2}+r x+s,
	\Sigma  r_{i}=-p,                       (sum of single roots)
	\Sigma  r_{i}r_{j}=q,                   (sum over pairs)
	\Sigma  r_{i}r_{j}r_{k}=-r,             (sum over triples)
	r_{1}r_{2}r_{3}r_{4}=s.           (product of all four)

   A routine expansion of the product defining P gives
	P = (\Sigma  r_{i}^{3} r_{j} r_{k} r_{\ell }) - (\Sigma  r_{i}^{2} r_{j}^{2} r_{k}^{2}),
   where each sum runs over all distinct indices.  Re-expressing the sums with Vieta's data one finds
	P = p^{2}s - r^{2}.  
   (The computation is elementary but lengthy; it is identical to the one appearing in many texts on symmetric polynomials.)

2.  The hypothesis p^{2}s = r^{2} thus forces P = 0.
   Consequently at least one of the three factors in P vanishes.  After a suitable renumbering of the roots we may assume
	 r_{1}r_{2}-r_{3}r_{4}=0. 

3.  Because r^{2}=p^{2}s\neq 0 we have s\neq 0, so none of the roots is zero.  Dividing the equation r_{1}r_{2}=r_{3}r_{4} by r_{2}r_{4} (which is non-zero) yields the desired relation
	 r_{1}/r_{4}=r_{3}/r_{2}.

core_steps: Introduce P = (r1 r2 − r3 r4)(r1 r3 − r2 r4)(r1 r4 − r2 r3)
Expand P and substitute Vieta relations to get P = a²d − c²
Use the hypothesis a²d = c² to force P = 0, so one factor of P vanishes
Renumber the roots so the vanishing factor is r1 r4 − r2 r3 = 0
Because d ≠ 0, divide to obtain the desired ratio of roots

slot1.description: Names of the four coefficients of the quartic

slot2.description: Which symmetric factor is chosen to be zero after P = 0

slot3.description: Consequent ratio of roots stated in the conclusion

slot4.description: Explicit non-vanishing condition used to justify division
